user_query:
Draft a polished technical guide for platform engineers on rolling out configuration drift detection using GitOps and policy-as-code. Read all inputs from input/:
- input/brief.md
- input/style_guide.md
- input/voice_samples/voice1.md
- input/voice_samples/voice2.md
- input/research.json
Deliver two files:
- output/article.md — the full article
- output/sources.json — a JSON object documenting every quantitative value you used and proving it came from input/research.json
Article requirements:
1) Voice and structure: Match the voice implied by the samples and style guide. Use a clear H1 title, then exactly these five H2 section headers in order:
   - ## The scene
   - ## Why drift hides
   - ## Implement the guardrails
   - ## Rollout plan
   - ## Takeaways
2) Open with something concrete: the very first non-empty paragraph must include a specific number (e.g., a metric) OR the article must start with a fenced code block.
3) Include at least two fenced code blocks (triple backticks) with practical examples.
4) Use the label "Operator's note:" at least once.
5) Integrate specific numbers from input/research.json verbatim (no rounding or inventing). Do not introduce any other quantitative claims not present in input/research.json.
6) The Takeaways section must include at least three bullet points.
7) Length: minimum 800 words.
8) Avoid banned phrases listed in the style guide.
Sources file requirements (output/sources.json):
- JSON with a key "numbers_used" that is an array of strings. Each string must be a verbatim quantitative value you used (e.g., "63%", "420 ms", "1200 ms", "11 of 18").
- Include at least four entries in numbers_used.
- Include a key "source_file" with the value "input/research.json".
- Every entry in numbers_used must appear as an exact substring in both output/article.md and input/research.json.
